title: IMGUI Performance
use_for: identifying repaint and layout hot paths
designing caching policy for GUIContent, styles, and labels
treating diagnostics dashboards and large-screen surfaces as hot paths from the start
planning resource reuse, binding invalidation cost, and subtree-level update efficiency
rules: treat OnGUI paths as hot paths until proven otherwise
avoid per-pass allocation of GUIContent, strings, and style helpers
keep layout structure stable to reduce hidden work
cache only where ownership and invalidation remain explicit
prefer prebuilt presentation resources over repeated regeneration
evaluate whether binding checks are cheaper than eager recomputation
assume diagnostics surfaces will face large-screen scaling pressure and dense repaint-sensitive content
procedure: Identify frequently repainted hosts and panels.
List per-pass allocations and repeated formatting work.
Move reusable GUI data into retained caches with explicit invalidation.
Simplify draw flow before adding optimization layers.

primary_when: the request is specifically about IMGUI runtime cost, allocations, repaint frequency, or caching strategy
must_not_own_when: the request is mainly about framework ownership or window composition

solves: where IMGUI frameworks usually pay avoidable allocation and repaint cost
how to optimize hot paths without hiding control flow
how to minimize the work done for local state changes by combining caches, bindings, and dirty propagation

core_principles: Favor simpler draw code before adding caches.
Cache only data with clear ownership and invalidation.
Do not trade event-flow clarity for micro-optimizations without evidence.
Spend CPU on targeted invalidation only when it costs less than rebuilding whole presentation paths.
Resource reuse, binding checks, and subtree updates should cooperate rather than compete.
constraints: Caching must not desynchronize visual output from retained state.
Per-pass string formatting and style construction are suspect by default.
High-frequency panels require tighter review than occasional tool windows.
Binding and dirty-check logic must remain cheaper than the work it is skipping.
Selective updates must not hide stale presentation state behind incorrect invalidation.
decision_rules: Add caching when the same labels, content, or style variants recur every pass.
Prefer stable host-level caches over many widget-local caches.
Instrument repaint and allocation behavior before inventing larger abstractions.
Use one-time resource generation for expensive derived presentation artifacts that change rarely.
Use subtree invalidation when the affected region is smaller than the cost of whole-host recomputation.
Attach imgui-adaptive-layout early when readability and density must survive resolution growth without exploding repaint cost.

preferred_patterns: explicit invalidation tied to state changes
small draw helpers with predictable data access
derived presentation caches keyed by stable identities and version stamps
incremental refresh paths that stop at clean subtree boundaries
avoid_patterns: string building inside loops every pass
optimizations that obscure where cost is actually incurred
binding systems that recompute every node each pass
resource regeneration that ignores whether inputs changed

recipes.name: Cache repeated labels for a frequently repainted panel
recipes.when: Use when the same text and icon combinations are emitted on every frame.
steps: Move label construction into retained cache owned by the host or panel state.
Invalidate the cache only when source data changes.
Keep cache population outside the hottest draw path where practical.
Restrict refresh to the panel or subtree whose bound data changed.

pitfalls: Blaming IMGUI broadly instead of isolating the real hot path.
Adding performance layers before stabilizing state and layout structure.
Using dirty propagation so broadly that everything is effectively always dirty.
Paying more for binding comparison than the skipped presentation work would have cost.
review_checklist: Are repeated allocations removed or justified?
Has the design assumed large-screen readability and scaling pressure early enough for diagnostics-style UI?
Does optimization preserve readable event flow?
Are expensive resources generated once and reused?
Can dirty propagation stop at subtree boundaries?
Are binding checks cheaper than the recomputation they avoid?

references.title: Local Reference: IMGUI Performance Guidelines
references.interpretation: Use this first for the domain's own hot-path rules, cache discipline, and allocation guidance.
references.why: Use as the primary local checklist for hot-path discipline, caching, and allocation control.

references.title: Local Reference: IMGUI Incremental Update Model
references.interpretation: Use this first when deciding how far resource reuse, bindings, and dirty propagation should go in the optimized update model.
references.why: Use for deciding when to rebuild, rebind, or stop at a clean subtree boundary.

references.title: Unity Manual: Immediate Mode GUI (IMGUI)
references.interpretation: Use this to confirm where IMGUI executes and why repeated draw-path work matters, not as a performance strategy by itself.
references.why: Use as the baseline for understanding where IMGUI executes and why hot-path discipline matters.
